refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total operational time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement for remote equipment control. Refinement clarifies the measurement basis (bidirectional link), acceptance threshold (0.999), and evaluation window. High safety criticality demands explicit availability targets to prevent loss-of-control scenarios.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during field trials; calculation of availability as (total_uptime / total_evaluation_period); comparison against 0.999 threshold with statistical confidence interval.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation or loss and alert the Safety Manager within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide redundant network paths or failover mechanisms to achieve 0.999 availability target.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single wireless link with best-effort QoS, no redundancy
DP-B: Dual independent network paths (primary cellular + secondary satellite or mesh) with automatic failover and link health monitoring
DP-C: Hybrid wired-wireless backbone with local mesh relay nodes and centralized link state management
DP-D: Software-defined networking (SDN) overlay with dynamic path selection and per-packet redundancy
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent network paths (primary cellular + secondary satellite or mesh) with automatic failover and link health monitoring. Primary path optimized for latency; secondary path optimized for coverage. Link state machine monitors both paths; failover triggered on primary degradation (latency > threshold or packet loss > 5%). Health telemetry reported via Telemetry interface.

potential_sources: Coupling to Vehicle power budget (secondary link may increase power draw)
Coupling to Safety Services alert latency (failover detection must complete within safety-critical timing)
Coupling to Teleoperation module's command/telemetry protocol design (must support dual-path multiplexing)
coupling_check.notes: DP-B is decoupled from specific wireless technology choice (cellular vs. satellite vs. mesh), allowing flexibility. Coupling to power and safety timing is manageable via interface contracts. Recommended: define link health SLA (latency, packet loss thresholds) and failover latency budget in DP refinement.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent network providers or technologies are available at the worksite.
Assumption: Secondary link has sufficient bandwidth for telemetry (may be lower than primary).
Risk: Failover latency may exceed operator reaction time if not carefully tuned; could cause loss-of-control transient.
Risk: Cost and complexity of dual-path infrastructure may exceed project budget.
Risk: Synchronization of command state across failover may introduce command duplication or loss.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to specify: (1) Primary/secondary link selection criteria and switching logic, (2) Failover latency budget (recommend ≤ 200 ms to maintain operator situational awareness), (3) Command/telemetry buffering and deduplication strategy during failover, (4) Link health metrics and thresholds (e.g., latency > 500 ms or packet loss > 5% triggers secondary path evaluation).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-path link management logic, failover state machine, link health monitoring, and command/telemetry multiplexing. Interfaces with Vehicle for command delivery and telemetry ingestion. Interfaces with Safety Services for link-loss alerts. Interfaces with external Network Link providers (primary and secondary).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode changes) from tele-operator console; Teleoperation module decodes and forwards to Vehicle via command link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link health metrics) from Vehicle to tele-operator console; Teleoperation module aggregates and formats telemetry from both primary and secondary paths.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Notifies Safety Services of link degradation, failover events, and link-loss conditions; triggers emergency stop or operator intervention protocols.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with primary and secondary network providers; Teleoperation module manages link state, monitors health, and executes failover logic.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link management logic, failover state machine, and command/telemetry multiplexing. High containment: all dual-path logic resides here. Low diffusion: only interfaces to Vehicle (command delivery) and Safety Services (alerts) are external. Recommended assignment.

Vehicle could host link management, but this would couple vehicle firmware to network topology and failover logic, reducing modularity. Vehicle should focus on command execution and telemetry generation, not link orchestration. Not recommended.
Safety Services should react to link-loss alerts, not own link management. Placing failover logic in Safety Services would blur safety vs. operational concerns. Not recommended.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; link management is a subsystem concern best owned by Teleoperation. Assigning to NGHE Solution would leave implementation ambiguous. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns all dual-path link management, failover logic, and link health monitoring. Vehicle module receives commands and sends telemetry without awareness of link redundancy. Safety Services receives link-loss alerts and triggers safety responses. This separation of concerns maximizes cohesion and minimizes coupling.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle firmware is updated independently, command/telemetry protocol changes could break Teleoperation's multiplexing logic. Mitigation: define stable command/telemetry interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle.

Risk: If network topology changes (e.g., secondary link provider changes), Teleoperation module must be reconfigured. Mitigation: parameterize link selection logic; use configuration files or APIs to specify primary/secondary providers.

Risk: Failover latency may exceed safety-critical timing if Teleoperation module is overloaded. Mitigation: allocate dedicated hardware or thread priority for link health monitoring and failover logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has: (1) Real-time or near-real-time link health monitoring (latency, packet loss, jitter), (2) Deterministic failover logic with bounded latency (≤ 200 ms), (3) Command/telemetry buffering to handle transient link loss, (4) Interface contracts with Vehicle (command/telemetry format) and Safety Services (alert format and latency SLA).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual-path failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve 0.999 link availability). Dual independent paths with automatic failover is a proven approach to achieve high availability. DP-B is technology-agnostic (allows cellular, satellite, mesh) and scalable. No decoupling violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic must be tightly coupled to link health monitoring; if monitoring is distributed across Vehicle and Teleoperation, state synchronization could fail. Mitigation: centralize link health monitoring in Teleoperation module.

Command/telemetry multiplexing during failover requires coordination with Vehicle; if Vehicle is unaware of failover, command duplication or loss could occur. Mitigation: define explicit failover handshake protocol between Teleoperation and Vehicle.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner of DP-B. All failover logic, link health monitoring, and multiplexing are contained within Teleoperation. Vehicle and Safety Services are consumers of Teleoperation's services, not participants in link management. Containment score 0.95 is justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-010 is feasible via field trials: deploy NGHE solution with dual-path network, monitor link state transitions over agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days), calculate availability as uptime / total_time, compare against 0.999 threshold. Teleoperation module must expose link health metrics (latency, packet loss, failover events) via Telemetry interface for verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) are allocated to Teleoperation and Vehicle as follows: FR-010.1 (command link) and FR-010.2 (telemetry link) are owned by Teleoperation (link management) and Vehicle (command/telemetry generation). FR-010.3 (link degradation alert) is owned by Teleoperation (detection) and Safety Services (response). FR-010.4 (redundancy) is owned by Teleoperation (failover logic).

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to specify failover latency budget (recommend ≤ 200 ms) and link health thresholds (latency, packet loss, jitter).
Define interface contracts between Teleoperation and Vehicle: command format, telemetry format, failover handshake protocol.
Define interface contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services: link-loss alert format, latency SLA (recommend ≤ 100 ms).
Allocate sub-FRs (FR-010.1 through 010.4) explicitly to Teleoperation and Vehicle modules.
Plan verification strategy: field trials with dual-path network, continuous link state monitoring, availability calculation, statistical confidence interval.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command link, telemetry link, link degradation alert, redundancy). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. Proceeding to next decomposition level is necessary to allocate requirements to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules and to define detailed design parameters (link health thresholds, failover latency, protocol specifications).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ 500 ms and packet loss ≤ 5%.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ 500 ms and packet loss ≤ 5%.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link degradation (latency > 500 ms or packet loss > 5%) and alert the Safety Manager within 100 ms.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide automatic failover from primary to secondary network path within 200 ms upon primary link degradation, maintaining command/telemetry continuity.

assumptions: Two independent network providers or technologies (e.g., cellular + satellite) are available at the worksite.
Secondary link has sufficient bandwidth for telemetry (may be lower than primary).
Operator can tolerate failover latency ≤ 200 ms without loss of situational awareness.
Vehicle firmware supports command/telemetry protocol compatible with dual-path multiplexing.
Safety Services can respond to link-loss alerts within safety-critical timing (e.g., trigger emergency stop within 500 ms).
open_risks: Failover latency may exceed operator reaction time if not carefully tuned; could cause loss-of-control transient.
Cost and complexity of dual-path infrastructure may exceed project budget.
Synchronization of command state across failover may introduce command duplication or loss.
Link health monitoring may introduce latency or jitter if not implemented with real-time constraints.
Secondary link provider may have different SLA or availability than primary; overall system availability may be limited by weaker link.
evidence_needed: Field trial data: link state transitions, failover events, latency/packet loss metrics over evaluation period.
Availability calculation: uptime / total_time, statistical confidence interval, comparison against 0.999 threshold.
Failover latency measurement: time from primary link degradation detection to secondary link activation.
Command/telemetry continuity verification: no command loss or duplication during failover.
Safety Services response time: time from link-loss alert to emergency stop or operator intervention.
Cost and complexity analysis: dual-path infrastructure cost, implementation effort, maintenance burden.
